chapters.title: French Alphabet
chapters.summary: Learn the 26 letters of the French alphabet, including accented characters and their names and pronunciations.

sections.title: The 26 Letters
sections.content: The French alphabet contains the same 26 letters as the English alphabet, but each letter has a distinct French name and pronunciation. Learning to name and pronounce each letter correctly is the essential first step to reading and speaking French.

sections.title: Accented Characters
sections.content: French uses several accent marks that change pronunciation or distinguish meaning. The key accented characters are: é (e-acute), è (e-grave), ê (e-circumflex), ë (e-dieresis), â (a-circumflex), ä (a-dieresis), î (i-circumflex), ï (i-dieresis), ô (o-circumflex), ù (u-grave), û (u-circumflex), ü (u-dieresis), ç (c-cedilla), and the ligatures œ (oe) and æ (ae).

sections.title: Letter Names
sections.content: Each French letter has a name used when spelling aloud. For example: A (ah), B (bay), C (say), D (day), E (euh), F (eff), G (zhay), H (ash), I (ee), J (zhee), K (kah), L (ell), M (em), N (en), O (oh), P (pay), Q (koo), R (air), S (ess), T (tay), U (oo), V (vay), W (doobluh-vay), X (eeks), Y (ee-grek), Z (zed).

sections.title: Alphabetical Order
sections.content: Alphabetical order in French follows the same sequence as English. When accented letters appear, they are treated as their base letter for alphabetical ordering. For example, é, è, ê all sort under E. The cedilla ç sorts under C.

sections.title: The French 'W'
sections.content: The letter W is almost exclusively used for foreign words like 'le week-end' or 'le wagon'. It is pronounced 'double-v'.

sections.title: Tricky Letters
sections.content: Pay special attention to G and J. In French, G is pronounced 'zhay' and J is pronounced 'zhee'. They are swapped compared to English!

examples.english: How do you spell it? C-H-A-T
examples.phonetics: kɔmɑ̃ sa seʁi — say, ash, ah, tay

examples.french: Mon prénom s'écrit avec un accent : André
examples.english: My first name is spelled with an accent: André
examples.phonetics: mɔ̃ pʁenɔ̃ seʁi avɛk œ̃n‿asɑ̃ : ɑ̃dʁe

examples.english: Jungle is spelled with a J.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a French accented character?
questions.explanation: ñ is a Spanish character, not French. French uses é, ç, œ and other accents.

questions.question: What is the French name for the letter W?
questions.explanation: W in French is called 'double-v' (doobluh-vay), literally 'double V'.

questions.question: Accented letters like é and è are treated as separate letters from E in alphabetical order.
questions.explanation: In French, accented letters sort under their base letter. É, è, ê all sort under E.

questions.question: The cedilla (ç) is used to:
options: Make the C sound like 's' before a, o, u
questions.correctAnswer: Make the C sound like 's' before a, o, u
questions.explanation: The cedilla (ç) changes the hard C sound to a soft S sound, especially before A, O, or U.

questions.question: How many letters are in the standard French alphabet (excluding accented variations)?
questions.explanation: The French alphabet has exactly 26 letters, the same as the English alphabet.

questions.question: How do you pronounce the letter 'G' in French?

chapters.title: French Pronunciation Rules
chapters.summary: Master the core differences between French and English sounds, including vowels, silent consonants, nasal vowels, and the distinctive French R.

sections.title: French Vowel Sounds vs English
sections.content: French vowels are 'pure' — unlike English vowels, they do not glide into another sound. The French A is always an open 'ah' sound, never the English diphthong in 'cake'. The French E has two main sounds: open (è) as in 'bet', and closed (é) as in a clipped 'ay' without the glide.

sections.title: Consonant Differences
sections.content: Several French consonants differ from their English equivalents. The French R is produced in the throat (a voiced uvular fricative), not with the tongue as in English. The French L is 'clear' with the tongue touching the teeth. The letters B, D, G, V at the end of words are sometimes voiced differently from English equivalents.

sections.title: Silent Final Consonants
sections.content: In French, most final consonants are silent. The word 'chat' (cat) sounds like 'sha' — the T is silent. Common silent final consonants: S, T, D, X, Z, P, G. Exceptions exist, especially in borrowed words. A useful rule: final C, R, F, L are often pronounced (think CaReFuL).

sections.title: Nasal Vowels
sections.content: French has four nasal vowel sounds, produced by allowing air to flow through both the mouth and nose. They occur when a vowel is followed by M or N: AN/EN (like 'ahn'), IN/IM (like a nasal 'uh'), ON/OM (like a nasal 'oh'), UN/UM (like a nasal 'uh' but rounder). When a double N or M follows (comme, bonne), the nasal effect disappears.

sections.title: French R — The Throat Sound
sections.content: The French R is one of the most recognisable sounds in the language. It is produced at the back of the throat (uvula) with a gentle vibration — similar to gargling. It is NOT the rolled R of Spanish, nor the English R. Practice by saying 'ah' then narrowing the throat slightly while exhaling.

sections.title: French U vs OU
sections.content: French has two distinct U sounds that English lacks. OU (as in 'ou', 'vous') sounds like the English 'oo' in 'food'. The pure French U (as in 'tu', 'sur') is a front-rounded vowel: shape your lips for 'oo' but try to say 'ee'. These two sounds must not be confused as they change word meaning.

sections.title: The French R
sections.content: The French 'R' is guttural, pronounced in the back of the throat. Think of it as a soft gargle or clearing your throat gently.

sections.title: Final Consonants
sections.content: Most final consonants (D, P, S, T, X, Z) are silent! 'Paris' is pronounced 'Paree'. Remember the acronym CaReFuL (C, R, F, L), which are usually pronounced at the end of words.

examples.english: a cat — the T and S are silent
the white wine — IN and AN are nasal vowels
red — R is a throat sound, G is silent
A grey cat. (The 't' and 's' are silent)
He is small. (The final 't' is silent)

questions.question: Which final consonants in French are most often SILENT?
questions.explanation: S, T, D, X, Z, P are typically silent at the end of French words. C, R, F, L are more often pronounced.

questions.question: French vowels glide into another sound, just like English vowels.
questions.explanation: French vowels are 'pure' — they hold a single sound without gliding, unlike English diphthongs.

questions.question: How many nasal vowel sounds does French have?
questions.explanation: French has 4 nasal vowels: AN/EN, IN/IM, ON/OM, and UN/UM.

questions.question: Where is the French R produced?
options: At the tip of the tongue
At the back of the throat (uvula)
At the roof of the mouth
questions.correctAnswer: At the back of the throat (uvula)
questions.explanation: The French R is a uvular sound, produced at the back of the throat — not like the English or Spanish R.

questions.question: What is the difference between 'tu' and 'tout' in French?
options: 'tu' uses the pure French U sound, 'tout' uses the OU sound
'tout' uses the pure French U sound, 'tu' uses OU
questions.correctAnswer: 'tu' uses the pure French U sound, 'tout' uses the OU sound
questions.explanation: 'tu' [ty] uses the front-rounded French U, while 'tout' [tu] uses the OU sound similar to English 'oo'.

questions.question: Which set of final consonants is usually pronounced in French?

chapters.title: Basic Reading Rules
chapters.summary: Connect sounds to spelling, decode unfamiliar French words, and master key phonetic patterns including silent E, vowel and consonant combinations.

sections.title: Sound to Spelling Connections
sections.content: French spelling is more consistent than English, though it still requires learning. Once you know the rules, you can read unfamiliar words aloud with reasonable accuracy. The key principle: each French letter or letter combination produces a predictable sound.

sections.title: Silent E
sections.content: A final E in French is usually silent: 'table' sounds like 'tabl'. However, E at the end of a syllable in the middle of a word is often pronounced as a short 'uh' (schwa): 'venu' [vəny]. In formal speech and singing, the E muet (mute E) is more often pronounced. In casual speech it is frequently dropped.

sections.title: Vowel Combinations
sections.content: Key vowel combinations to learn: AU and EAU both sound like 'oh' (eau, chaud). OU sounds like 'oo' (bonjour, vous). UI sounds like 'wee' (nuit, lui). AI and EI sound like 'eh' (faire, neige). EU sounds like a rounded 'uh' (feu, deux). OI sounds like 'wah' (moi, toi, voici).

sections.title: Consonant Combinations
sections.content: Essential consonant combinations: CH sounds like English 'sh' (chat, chercher). GN sounds like 'ny' (gagner, oignon, agneau). PH sounds like 'f' (photo, pharmacie). QU sounds like 'k' (qui, que, question). TH sounds like 't' in French (not as in English 'the') — théâtre, thé.

sections.title: Decoding Unfamiliar Words
sections.content: A strategy for reading new French words: identify vowel groups first, then apply consonant rules. Remember that final consonants are mostly silent. Use known word parts — many French and English words share Latin roots. When in doubt about stress, French stress falls on the final syllable of a word or rhythmic group.

sections.title: Vowel Combinations (AU/EAU)
sections.content: When you see 'au' or 'eau', pronounce it as a closed 'o' (like in 'go', without the glide). Example: 'beau' = [bo].

sections.title: Vowel Combinations (OU)
sections.content: The combination 'ou' is pronounced like the 'oo' in 'soup'. Example: 'vous' = [vu].

questions.question: What sound does the combination CH make in French?
questions.explanation: In French, CH makes the 'sh' sound — so 'chat' sounds like 'sha', not 'chat' as in English.

questions.question: The vowel combination OI in French sounds like:
questions.explanation: OI makes a 'wah' sound: moi [mwa], toi [twa], voici [vwasi].

questions.question: The final E in French words like 'table' is always pronounced clearly.
questions.explanation: Final E in French is usually silent — 'table' sounds like 'tabl'. It is the E muet (mute E).

questions.question: The combination PH in French sounds like:
questions.explanation: PH in French makes an 'f' sound: photo, pharmacie, téléphone.

questions.question: Both AU and EAU in French sound like:
questions.explanation: AU and EAU both produce the 'oh' sound: chaud [ʃo], eau [o], beau [bo].

questions.question: How is 'eau' pronounced?

chapters.title: Pre-A1 Readiness Checkpoint
chapters.summary: Diagnostic review of Pre-A1 content: alphabet recognition, pronunciation identification, and reading unfamiliar words aloud.

sections.title: What This Checkpoint Covers
sections.content: This diagnostic review tests your mastery of all three Pre-A1 topics: the French alphabet and accented characters, French pronunciation rules (vowels, consonants, nasals, the French R), and basic reading rules (phonetic patterns, vowel/consonant combinations). Use your results to identify any gaps before beginning A1 grammar.

sections.title: Preparing for A1
sections.content: Now that you know the alphabet and reading rules, you are ready to start associating these sounds with actual vocabulary and grammar structures.

questions.question: Which word contains a nasal vowel sound?
questions.explanation: 'brun' (brown) contains the nasal UN vowel sound. The UN/UM combination creates a nasal vowel.

questions.question: How would you read the word 'oignon' aloud in French?
questions.explanation: GN makes the 'ny' sound, and the final ON is nasal. So 'oignon' = [ɔɲɔ̃], roughly 'oh-nyɔ̃'.

questions.question: The letter H in French words like 'hôtel' is always pronounced.
questions.explanation: H in French is almost always silent. 'Hôtel' is pronounced [otɛl] — the H is mute.

questions.question: Which French letter combination sounds like the English 'f'?
questions.explanation: PH in French makes the 'f' sound, just as in English. CH='sh', GN='ny', QU='k'.

questions.question: True or False: Final 'S' is almost always silent.

chapters.title: Liaison and Elision
chapters.summary: Learn when to link words with liaison, how elision replaces vowels before other vowels, and master common fixed liaison phrases.

sections.title: What Is Liaison?
sections.content: Liaison is the linking of a normally silent final consonant to the following word when it begins with a vowel or silent H. For example, 'les amis' — the S in 'les' is normally silent, but before 'amis' (which starts with a vowel), it is pronounced as a Z sound: [lɛz‿ami]. Liaison makes French sound smooth and flowing.

sections.title: Required Liaison
sections.content: Liaison is required (mandatory) in several contexts: between a determiner and a noun (les amis, ces enfants), between an adjective and a following noun (un grand arbre), between a pronoun and its verb (nous_avons, vous_êtes, ils_ont), after common one-syllable prepositions (en_été, chez_eux), and in numbers before nouns (deux_amis, trois_heures).

sections.title: Forbidden Liaison
sections.content: Liaison is forbidden in certain positions: after a singular noun (un étudiant / intelligent — no liaison), before an H aspiré (un / hibou — no liaison), after the word 'et' (lui et / elle — no liaison), and between a plural subject noun and its verb (les étudiants / arrivent — no liaison in writing-based speech).

sections.title: Elision — Vowel Dropping
sections.content: Elision occurs when certain small words ending in E or A drop their final vowel before a word beginning with a vowel or silent H. The dropped vowel is replaced by an apostrophe. Key words that undergo elision: je → j' (j'aime), me → m' (il m'appelle), te → t' (il t'attend), le/la → l' (l'ami, l'amie), de → d' (d'accord), que → qu' (qu'est-ce que), ne → n' (n'est-ce pas), ce → c' (c'est).

sections.title: Common Fixed Liaison Phrases
sections.content: Some liaisons appear so frequently they are best learned as fixed phrases: Comment allez-vous [kɔmɑ̃t‿ale vu], Vous avez [vuz‿ave], C'est un [sɛt‿œ̃], De temps en temps [də tɑ̃z‿ɑ̃ tɑ̃], de plus en plus [də plyz‿ɑ̃ ply]. In these set expressions, liaison is always required.

sections.title: Mandatory Liaison
sections.content: Liaison is mandatory between a pronoun and a verb (Ils_ont), and an article and a noun (Les_amis).

sections.title: Forbidden Liaison
sections.content: Never make a liaison after 'et' (and), or before an aspirated 'h' (les / haricots).

examples.english: the friends — liaison: S links as Z
I love chocolate — elision: je → j' before vowel
we have — required liaison between pronoun and verb
The school is big — elision: la → l' before vowel
A great man. (T sound liaison from grand)

questions.question: In the phrase 'les enfants', the S in 'les' is pronounced because of:
questions.explanation: Between a determiner (les) and a noun starting with a vowel (enfants), liaison is required. The S sounds like Z.

questions.question: Which word undergoes elision before a vowel?
questions.explanation: 'je' becomes 'j'' before a vowel: j'aime, j'arrive. The other pronouns (nous, vous, ils) do not elide.

questions.question: Liaison is required after the word 'et' (and).
questions.explanation: Liaison is FORBIDDEN after 'et'. 'Lui et elle' — no liaison between 'et' and 'elle'.

questions.question: How would you write 'de accord' with correct elision?
questions.explanation: 'de' elides to 'd'' before a vowel. 'D'accord' (OK/agreed) uses elision of the preposition 'de'.

questions.question: In 'vous êtes', the liaison S sounds like which consonant?
questions.explanation: When a final S links through liaison, it is always pronounced as a Z sound: vous êtes [vuz‿ɛt].

questions.question: What sound does the 's' make in 'Les amis'?

chapters.title: Noun Gender
chapters.summary: Understand the masculine and feminine gender system in French, and learn clues from noun endings to help predict gender.

sections.title: The Masculine/Feminine Concept
sections.content: Every French noun has a grammatical gender — either masculine (masculin) or feminine (féminin). Unlike in English, this applies to all nouns including objects, ideas, and abstractions — not just people or animals. Gender affects the form of articles, adjectives, and pronouns used with the noun. There is no neutral gender in French.

sections.title: Gender Clues from Noun Endings
sections.content: While gender must be learned for each noun, certain endings are strong indicators. Typically FEMININE endings: -tion (la nation, la question), -sion (la passion), -ité (la liberté), -ette (la baguette), -ence/-ance (la patience, la chance), -ure (la culture), -ée (la journée). Typically MASCULINE endings: -age (le voyage, le fromage), -ment (le gouvernement, le document), -eur (le directeur), -isme (le tourisme), -et (le buffet).

sections.title: Important Exceptions
sections.content: Rules have exceptions. Some -age words are feminine: la plage (beach), la cage. Some -eur words are feminine: la fleur (flower), la chaleur (heat), la peur (fear), la douleur (pain). Some common words break patterns entirely: le problème (masculine despite -ème), le musée (masculine despite -ée). Always learn the article with the noun.

sections.title: Gender Tips: Endings
sections.content: Words ending in -tion, -sion, -té, -ette, and -ence are almost always feminine (la nation, la liberté). Words ending in -ment, -age, -eau are usually masculine (le fromage, le bateau).

sections.title: Exceptions
sections.content: There are always exceptions. 'L'eau' (water) ends in -eau but is feminine. 'Le squelette' (skeleton) ends in -ette but is masculine!

examples.french: la nation, la question, la solution
examples.english: the nation, the question, the solution — all feminine, ending in -tion
examples.phonetics: la nasjɔ̃, la kɛstjɔ̃, la solysjɔ̃

examples.french: le voyage, le fromage, le mariage
examples.english: the trip, the cheese, the marriage — all masculine, ending in -age
examples.phonetics: lə vwajaʒ, lə fʁɔmaʒ, lə maʁjaʒ

examples.english: the beach — exception: feminine despite -age ending

questions.question: A noun ending in -tion in French is most likely:
questions.explanation: Nouns ending in -tion are almost always feminine in French: la nation, la question, la solution.

questions.question: Which of the following is an exception to the -age = masculine rule?
questions.explanation: 'La plage' (the beach) is feminine despite ending in -age, which is usually masculine.

questions.question: In French, only nouns referring to people and animals have grammatical gender.
questions.explanation: ALL French nouns have grammatical gender — objects, ideas, and abstractions too. Every noun is either masculine or feminine.

questions.question: The noun 'liberté' (freedom) — what is its likely gender based on the ending?
questions.explanation: -ité and -té endings indicate feminine gender: la liberté, la beauté, la vérité.

questions.question: What is the best strategy for learning French noun gender?
options: Always guess masculine — it is more common
Learn the article with every noun from the start
Only learn gender rules and apply them
Gender does not matter in everyday speech
questions.correctAnswer: Learn the article with every noun from the start
questions.explanation: Because exceptions exist, the most reliable approach is to always learn the article (le/la) together with each new noun.

questions.question: A noun ending in '-tion' is usually...

chapters.title: Singular and Plural Nouns
chapters.summary: Learn how to form plural nouns in French, including regular patterns, special endings, and key irregular forms.

sections.title: The Default Plural — Adding S
sections.content: The most common way to form the plural in French is to add S to the singular noun: un chat → des chats, une maison → des maisons. Important: this S is usually silent in pronunciation. The plural is signalled primarily by the article, not by the noun's sound.

sections.title: Nouns Unchanged in Plural
sections.content: Nouns already ending in S, X, or Z do not change in the plural: un bras → des bras (arms), une voix → des voix (voices), un nez → des nez (noses). The plural is shown only by the article.

sections.title: Special Plural Endings
sections.content: Several patterns require special treatment: Nouns ending in -EAU or -EU add X (not S): un gâteau → des gâteaux, un jeu → des jeux. Nouns ending in -AL change to -AUX: un animal → des animaux, un cheval → des chevaux, un journal → des journaux. Nouns ending in -OU: most add S (un trou → des trous), but seven common ones add X: bijou, caillou, chou, genou, hibou, joujou, pou.

sections.title: Plural Pronunciation
sections.content: A crucial point: the plural S added to nouns is almost always silent. 'Chat' and 'chats' sound identical in isolation. The plural meaning is communicated through the article: 'le chat' (singular) vs 'les chats' (plural). The S becomes audible only through liaison: 'les_animaux'.

sections.title: Plurals in -X
sections.content: Nouns ending in -eu, -au, or -eau take an 'x' in the plural, not an 's'. Example: un bateau -> des bateaux. Un feu -> des feux.

sections.title: Invariable Nouns
sections.content: Nouns that already end in -s, -x, or -z do not change in the plural. Example: un nez (a nose) -> des nez (noses). Un choix -> des choix.

examples.english: a cake → cakes — -eau becomes -eaux in plural
a voice → voices — X ending unchanged
The boats are on the water.

questions.question: What is the plural of 'un cheval' (a horse)?
questions.explanation: Nouns ending in -AL change to -AUX in the plural: cheval → chevaux.

questions.question: The plural S added to French nouns is almost always silent in pronunciation.
questions.explanation: 'chat' and 'chats' sound identical. The plural is signalled by the article (le vs les), not the noun sound.

questions.question: Nouns already ending in S, X, or Z in the singular:
questions.explanation: Words ending in S, X, Z do not change: un bras → des bras, une voix → des voix.

questions.question: What is the plural of 'un jeu' (a game)?
questions.explanation: Nouns ending in -EU add X: un jeu → des jeux, un feu → des feux.

questions.question: What is the plural of 'un château'?

chapters.title: Subject Pronouns
chapters.summary: Learn all nine French subject pronouns, the difference between tu and vous, how on replaces nous in speech, and agreement with ils.

sections.title: The Nine Subject Pronouns
sections.content: French has nine subject pronouns: je (I), tu (you — informal singular), il (he/it), elle (she/it), on (one/we — informal), nous (we — formal), vous (you — formal or plural), ils (they — masculine or mixed), elles (they — feminine only). Unlike English, French verbs change form for each pronoun.

sections.title: Tu vs Vous — Formal and Informal
sections.content: French has two words for 'you'. Tu is the informal singular, used with friends, family, children, and peers. Vous is used in two situations: (1) as a formal/polite singular when addressing strangers, professionals, elders, or anyone you want to show respect to; (2) as the ordinary plural for two or more people regardless of formality. Using tu when vous is expected is a social mistake. When in doubt, start with vous.

sections.title: On — The Informal 'We'
sections.content: On is technically an impersonal pronoun meaning 'one' or 'people in general', but in everyday spoken French it is the standard way to say 'we'. 'On mange à midi' means 'We eat at noon.' On always takes a third-person singular verb form, even when it means 'we'. This is one of the most important features of spoken French.

sections.title: Ils for Mixed Gender Groups
sections.content: When referring to a group of people or things that includes at least one masculine element, French uses ils (masculine plural). Elles is only used when ALL members of the group are feminine. A group of ten women and one man = ils. This is called the 'masculine generic' rule in French grammar.

sections.title: Tu vs Vous Basics
sections.content: French has two ways to say 'you'. 'Tu' is singular and informal (friends, kids, pets). 'Vous' is plural (y'all), AND singular formal (strangers, professors, elders).

examples.french: Tu parles français ? vs Vous parlez français ?
examples.english: Do you speak French? (informal) vs Do you speak French? (formal/plural)
examples.phonetics: ty paʁl fʁɑ̃sɛ vs vu paʁle fʁɑ̃sɛ

examples.french: On va au restaurant ce soir.
examples.english: We're going to the restaurant tonight. (casual speech)
examples.phonetics: ɔ̃ va o ʁɛstɔʁɑ̃ sə swaʁ

examples.french: Marc et Marie ? Ils sont au café.
examples.english: Marc and Marie? They're at the café. — mixed group uses ils
examples.phonetics: maʁk e maʁi ? il sɔ̃t‿o kafe

questions.question: You are meeting your doctor for the first time. Which pronoun do you use to address them?
questions.explanation: Use 'vous' with strangers, professionals, and anyone you want to show respect. Tu is for people you know well.

questions.question: In casual French, 'on' is commonly used to mean 'we'.
questions.explanation: 'On mange ensemble ?' (Shall we eat together?) — using 'on' for 'we' is standard in everyday spoken French.

questions.question: A group of 8 women and 2 men is referred to in French as:
questions.explanation: Any mixed-gender group (even one man among many women) uses 'ils' — the masculine plural form.

questions.question: How many subject pronouns does French have?
questions.explanation: French has 9 subject pronouns: je, tu, il, elle, on, nous, vous, ils, elles.

questions.question: Which pronoun do you use when speaking to your boss?

chapters.title: On as Informal We
chapters.summary: Deep dive into using 'on' as casual 'we' in spoken French, verb agreement, fixed expressions, and register awareness.

sections.title: On Replacing Nous in Casual Speech
sections.content: In everyday conversational French, 'on' has almost completely replaced 'nous' as the pronoun for 'we'. 'Nous allons' is still correct and used in writing, but natives say 'on va'. This shift is so complete that using 'nous' in casual speech can sound unnatural or overly formal.

sections.title: Verb Agreement with On
sections.content: When 'on' means 'we', the verb still takes the 3rd person singular form — the same form as 'il/elle'. 'On mange' (we eat), 'on est allés' (we went). In the passé composé with être, adjective agreement can optionally reflect the actual subjects: 'On est contents' (we, masculine plural, are happy) is acceptable in informal writing.

sections.title: Fixed Expressions with On
sections.content: Several important fixed expressions use 'on': On y va! (Let's go!), On verra (We'll see), On dirait que (It looks as if / You'd think), On sait jamais (You never know — informal), Comme on dit (As they say).

sections.title: On vs Ils/Elles
sections.content: While 'on' usually means 'we', it can also mean 'one' or 'people in general'. Example: 'En France, on mange du fromage' (In France, people eat cheese).

examples.english: Are we eating together tonight? — on = we in casual speech
Let's go! — fixed expression with on

questions.question: In casual French, which sentence sounds more natural?
questions.explanation: In casual spoken French, 'on va' (we're going) is far more natural than 'nous allons', which sounds formal.

questions.question: What verb form does 'on' always take?
questions.explanation: Even when 'on' means 'we', it always uses the 3rd person singular verb form: on mange, on est, on fait.

questions.question: What verb conjugation does 'On' take?

chapters.title: Definite Articles
chapters.summary: Master le, la, l', and les — the French definite articles — including contractions with à and de.

sections.title: Le, La, L', Les
sections.content: French has four forms of the definite article: le (masculine singular), la (feminine singular), l' (before vowel or silent H, any gender), les (plural, any gender). They are used for specific or known nouns and for general categories — more broadly than English 'the'.

sections.title: Specific vs General Use
sections.content: Unlike English, French uses the definite article for general preferences and universal truths: 'J'aime le chocolat' (I love chocolate — in general, not a specific piece), 'Les chats sont indépendants' (Cats are independent — as a species). English drops the article in these cases; French requires it.

sections.title: Contraction with À — Au and Aux
sections.content: When 'à' (to/at) is followed by a masculine singular article (le), they contract to 'au'. When followed by les, they contract to 'aux'. There is no contraction with la or l'. Examples: à + le = au (Je vais au marché), à + les = aux (Je parle aux étudiants), à + la = à la (Je vais à la boulangerie), à + l' = à l' (Je vais à l'hôpital).

sections.title: Contraction with De — Du and Des
sections.content: When 'de' (of/from) is followed by a masculine singular article (le), they contract to 'du'. When followed by les, they contract to 'des'. There is no contraction with la or l'. Examples: de + le = du (le livre du professeur), de + les = des (le livre des étudiants), de + la = de la (la porte de la maison), de + l' = de l' (la porte de l'école).

sections.title: Generalizations with Definite Articles
sections.content: Unlike English, French requires definite articles (le, la, les) when making general statements about likes/dislikes. 'I like apples' becomes 'J'aime *les* pommes'.

examples.french: J'aime le café et la musique.
examples.english: I like coffee and music. (general preference — definite article used)
examples.phonetics: ʒɛm lə kafe e la myzik

examples.french: Je vais au marché. / Je parle aux enfants.
examples.english: I'm going to the market. / I'm talking to the children.
examples.phonetics: ʒə vɛ o maʁʃe / ʒə paʁl oz‿ɑ̃fɑ̃

examples.english: the teacher's book (book of the teacher)

questions.question: Which article is used before a masculine singular noun starting with a vowel?
questions.explanation: Before any vowel or silent H (masculine or feminine), 'le' and 'la' both become 'l'': l'ami, l'amie, l'hôtel.

questions.question: How do you say 'I'm going to the cinema' in French? (le cinéma)
questions.explanation: à + le contracts to 'au': je vais au cinéma. You cannot say 'à le' in French.

questions.question: In French, 'J'aime le chocolat' means 'I like the (specific) chocolate.'
questions.explanation: In French, the definite article is used for general preferences. 'J'aime le chocolat' means 'I like chocolate' in general.

questions.question: Complete: 'le livre ___ étudiants' (the students' book)
questions.explanation: de + les contracts to 'des': le livre des étudiants. You cannot say 'de les' in French.

questions.question: Translate: 'I like dogs.'

chapters.title: Indefinite Articles
chapters.summary: Master the use of un, une, and des to refer to non-specific items and people.

sections.title: Forms of the Indefinite Article
sections.content: French has three indefinite articles: 'un' (masculine singular), 'une' (feminine singular), and 'des' (plural). Unlike English 'a/an', the French article must match the gender and number of the noun it precedes.

sections.title: Using 'Des' for Plurals
sections.content: In English, we often omit the article for plurals (e.g., 'I have books'). In French, you must use 'des': 'J'ai des livres.' It translates to 'some' or just indicates that there are multiple items.

sections.title: Negative Sentences: De/D'
sections.content: In negative sentences, un, une, and des usually change to 'de' (or 'd'' before a vowel). Example: 'J'ai un chat' -> 'Je n'ai pas de chat.' This rule applies to all indefinite articles to show zero quantity.

sections.title: Indefinite Articles in Negative Sentences
sections.content: In a negative sentence, 'un', 'une', and 'des' usually change to 'de' or 'd' (before a vowel). 'J'ai un livre' -> 'Je n'ai pas de livre'.

sections.title: Des vs De (+ Adjective)
sections.content: When a plural adjective comes before a noun, 'des' often changes to 'de'. Example: 'De belles fleurs' (rather than 'Des belles fleurs').

questions.question: Which article is used for a feminine singular noun?
What does 'un' change to in a negative sentence (e.g., 'Je n'ai pas...')?
How does 'un' change in a negative sentence?

chapters.title: Adjective Agreement and Placement
chapters.summary: Learn how adjectives must agree in gender and number with nouns, and where to place them (the BAGS rule).

sections.title: Gender and Number Agreement
sections.content: Adjectives in French must match the noun they describe. Usually, you add 'e' for feminine and 's' for plural. Example: un chat noir (m.) -> une chatte noire (f.).

sections.title: Standard Placement (After the Noun)
sections.content: Most French adjectives follow the noun: 'un livre intéressant'. This is the opposite of English word order.

sections.title: The BAGS Rule (Before the Noun)
sections.content: A small group of frequent adjectives comes BEFORE the noun. Use the acronym BAGS: Beauty (beau, joli), Age (vieux, jeune, nouveau), Goodness (bon, mauvais), and Size (grand, petit).

sections.title: BANGS Adjectives
sections.content: Most French adjectives go AFTER the noun. However, adjectives in the BANGS category (Beauty, Age, Number, Goodness, Size) go BEFORE. Examples: beau, jeune, deux, bon, grand.

sections.title: Adjectives with Dual Meanings
sections.content: Some adjectives change meaning based on position. 'Un grand homme' (A great man) vs 'Un homme grand' (A tall man).

examples.english: A small red car. (Petit before, rouge after)
A small black cat. (Small = before, Black = after)

questions.question: Which type of adjective usually comes BEFORE the noun?
Where does the adjective 'rouge' (red) usually go?

chapters.title: Partitive Articles
chapters.summary: Understand how to use partitive articles (some/any) for uncountable items like food and liquids.

sections.title: Du, De la, De l'
sections.content: Partitive articles are used for part of a whole or uncountable quantities. 'Du' (masculine), 'De la' (feminine), 'De l'' (before vowel).

sections.title: Partitive vs Definite
sections.content: Use partitive for quantity (I want some coffee: 'Je veux du café') but definite for preference (I like coffee: 'J'aime le café').

sections.title: Partitive vs Indefinite
sections.content: Use partitive articles (du, de la, de l') for uncountable things (food, liquid, abstract concepts like courage). Use indefinite (un/une) for countable things.

sections.title: Negatives with Partitives
sections.content: Like indefinite articles, partitives change to 'de' in the negative. 'Je mange du pain' -> 'Je ne mange pas de pain'.

examples.french: Je voudrais de l'eau, s'il vous plaît.
examples.english: I would like some water, please.

questions.question: Which article is used for 'some coffee' (café is masculine)?
Which article is used for 'some coffee' (masculine)?

chapters.title: Verb Être — Present Tense
chapters.summary: Master the most important verb in French: Être (To Be).

sections.title: Conjugation of Être
sections.content: Je suis, Tu es, Il/Elle/On est, Nous sommes, Vous êtes, Ils/Elles sont.

sections.title: Common Uses
sections.content: Être is used for nationality, profession, adjectives/descriptions, and as an auxiliary in past tenses.

sections.title: Être in Fixed Expressions
sections.content: Être is used for nationality, profession, and religion. Note: No article is used for professions after être! 'Il est médecin' (He is a doctor).

sections.title: Describing State vs Personality
sections.content: Être is also used for temporary states and permanent personality traits. 'Je suis fatigué' (I am tired). 'Elle est intelligente' (She is intelligent).

questions.question: What is the 'vous' form of être?
What is the 'nous' form of 'être'?

chapters.title: Verb Avoir — Present Tense
chapters.summary: Learn the verb Avoir (To Have) and its many idiomatic physical/emotional expressions.

sections.title: Conjugation of Avoir
sections.content: J'ai, Tu as, Il/Elle/On a, Nous avons, Vous avez, Ils/Elles ont.

sections.title: Avoir Expressions
sections.content: In French, we use 'avoir' for things English uses 'be' for: Avoir faim (to be hungry), Avoir soif (to be thirsty), Avoir peur (to be afraid), Avoir [number] ans (to be [age] old).

sections.title: Avoir for Physical Feelings
sections.content: French uses 'avoir' where English uses 'to be' for physical states like hunger, thirst, cold, and heat. 'J'ai faim' (I have hunger = I am hungry).

sections.title: Avoir for Age
sections.content: In French, you *have* an age, you aren't an age. 'J'ai vingt ans' (I have 20 years = I am 20).

questions.question: How do you say 'I am hungry' in French?
How do you say 'I am 25 years old' in French?

chapters.title: C'est vs Il Est
chapters.summary: Solve the eternal confusion between C'est and Il est for identifying and describing things.

sections.title: C'est + Noun
sections.content: Use 'C'est' (or 'Ce sont') to identify people or objects followed by a modified noun. Example: 'C'est un professeur.' 'C'est ma voiture.'

sections.title: Il est + Adjective
sections.content: Use 'Il/Elle est' to describe a person's profession or nationality directly (without an article) or to describe someone with an adjective. Example: 'Il est professeur.' 'Elle est intelligente.'

examples.french: C'est mon ami Marc. Il est très sympa.
examples.english: This is my friend Marc. He is very nice.

questions.question: Which is correct to say 'He is French'?

chapters.title: Capitalisation Rules
chapters.summary: Avoid common pitfalls: French capitalisation differs significantly from English.

sections.title: Days and Months
sections.content: Unlike English, days of the week (lundi, mardi...) and months (janvier, février...) are NOT capitalised in French unless they start a sentence.

sections.title: Nationalities and Languages
sections.content: Languages (le français, l'anglais) are never capitalised. Nationalities are capitalised as NOUNS (Un Français) but NOT as ADJECTIVES (Un film français).

examples.english: I speak French on Mondays. (No caps for language or day)
He is an Italian man. (italien is an adjective here, no cap)

chapters.title: Regular ER Verbs — Present
chapters.summary: Learn the most common verb group in French: the regular -ER verbs.

sections.title: The Pattern
sections.content: Drop the -ER ending and add: -e, -es, -e, -ons, -ez, -ent. Verb: Parler (to speak) -> Je parle, tu parles, il parle, nous parlons, vous parlez, ils parlent.

sections.title: Spelling Changes in -ER Verbs
sections.content: Verbs ending in -ger (like manger) keep the 'e' before 'o' in the nous form: 'mangeons'. Verbs in -cer (like lancer) add a cedilla: 'lançons'.

sections.title: Stem-changing ER Verbs
sections.content: Verbs like 'acheter' add an accent in some forms: 'j'achète'. Verbs like 'appeler' double the consonant: 'j'appelle'.

questions.question: What is the ending for 'nous' in ER verbs?
What is the 'nous' form of 'manger'?

chapters.title: Regular IR Verbs — Present
chapters.summary: Master the second regular group: the -IR verbs (Finir type).

sections.title: The -ISS- Pattern
sections.content: Regular -IR verbs like Finir add -iss- in the plural forms. Endings: -is, -is, -it, -issons, -issez, -issent.

questions.question: Conjugate 'choisir' for 'nous'.

chapters.title: Regular RE Verbs — Present
chapters.summary: Conquer the third regular group: the -RE verbs (Vendre type).

sections.title: The Pattern
sections.content: Drop the -RE and add: -s, -s, -(no ending), -ons, -ez, -ent. Verb: Vendre (to sell) -> Je vends, tu vends, il vend, nous vendons...

examples.english: I am waiting for the bus.

chapters.title: Negation with Ne Pas
chapters.summary: Learn the standard 'sandwich' rule for negation using ne... pas, and the transition of articles in negative sentences.

sections.title: The Ne... Pas Sandwich
sections.content: To make a sentence negative, place 'ne' before the conjugated verb and 'pas' after it. Example: 'Je mange' -> 'Je ne mange pas.' If the verb starts with a vowel, 'ne' becomes 'n''. Example: 'J'aime' -> 'Je n'aime pas.'

sections.title: Articles in Negation
sections.content: A crucial rule: indefinite articles (un, une, des) and partitive articles (du, de la, des) change to 'de' or 'd'' in a negative sentence. Example: 'J'ai un livre' -> 'Je n'ai pas de livre.' 'Je bois du café' -> 'Je ne bois pas de café.'

sections.title: Negation with Vowels (Ne -> N')
sections.content: When the verb starts with a vowel or silent h, 'ne' becomes 'n'. 'Je n'aime pas'. 'Il n'habite pas'.

sections.title: Placement with Compound Tenses
sections.content: In the Passé Composé, ne...pas surrounds the auxiliary verb. 'Je n'ai pas mangé'.

examples.french: Il ne travaille pas le dimanche.

examples.english: We don't want to go out.

questions.question: Where does 'pas' go in a sentence with a single conjugated verb?
options: At the end of the sentence

questions.question: Where does 'pas' go in 'Je ne parle pas'?

chapters.title: Basic Question Formation
chapters.summary: Learn the two formal ways to ask questions: using 'Est-ce que' and inversion.

sections.title: Using 'Est-ce que'
sections.content: The simplest way to form a question is to add 'Est-ce que' (literally 'Is it that') to the beginning of a statement. The word order remains the same. Example: 'Tu parles' -> 'Est-ce que tu parles ?'

sections.title: The Inversion Method
sections.content: In formal French, you can swap the subject pronoun and the verb, joining them with a hyphen. Example: 'Tu parles' -> 'Parles-tu ?' If the verb ends in a vowel and the pronoun is il/elle/on, add a '-t-' for pronunciation: 'A-t-il faim ?'

sections.title: Inversion in Questions
sections.content: The most formal way to ask a question is by inverting the subject and verb. 'Parlez-vous français ?' If a third-person verb ends in a vowel, add a '-t-' between the verb and pronoun: 'Parle-t-il ?'.

sections.title: Est-ce que Usage
sections.content: Using 'Est-ce que' is the standard way to turn any statement into a question without changing word order. 'Est-ce que tu parles français ?'.

questions.question: What happens to 'Est-ce que' before a word starting with a vowel?
How do you form a formal question with 'tu' and 'parles'?

chapters.title: Informal Questions
chapters.summary: Ask questions like a native using intonation and informal tag words.

sections.title: Rising Intonation
sections.content: In casual conversation, you don't need 'Est-ce que' or inversion. Simply say the statement with a rising pitch at the end. Example: 'Tu viens ?' (You're coming?)

sections.title: Tag Questions
sections.content: Use 'n'est-ce pas ?' at the end of a statement to ask for confirmation, similar to 'right?' or 'isn't it?'. Example: 'Il fait beau, n'est-ce pas ?'

chapters.title: Numbers 1–69
chapters.summary: Master the foundation of French numbers from 1 to 69.

sections.title: Counting to Twenty
sections.content: 1: un, 2: deux, 3: trois, 4: quatre, 5: cinq, 6: six, 7: sept, 8: huit, 9: neuf, 10: dix. 11: onze, 12: douze, 13: treize, 14: quatorze, 15: quinze, 16: seize, 17: dix-sept, 18: dix-huit, 19: dix-neuf, 20: vingt.

sections.title: The Tens (up to 60)
sections.content: 30: trente, 40: quarante, 50: cinquante, 60: soixante. For numbers like 21, 31, etc., use 'et un': vingt-et-un. For 22-29, use a hyphen: vingt-deux.

sections.title: The Number 21 Trick
sections.content: For numbers ending in 1 (21, 31, 41, 51, 61), French uses 'et un' (and one). 'Vingt et un'. For other numbers, use a hyphen: 'vingt-deux'.

sections.title: Pronunciation of Numbers
sections.content: The 'x' in six and dix is pronounced 's' when the number is alone, but it becomes silent before a noun starting with a consonant: 'six pommes' [si pɔm].

questions.question: How do you say 21 in French?

chapters.title: Numbers 70–99
chapters.summary: Conquer the unique French logic for numbers 70-99 (Vigesimal system).

sections.title: The 70s and 90s
sections.content: French doesn't have a unique word for 70 or 90 in most dialects. 70 is 'soixante-dix' (sixty-ten). 71 is 'soixante-et-onze'. 90 is 'quatre-vingt-dix' (four-twenty-ten).

sections.title: The 80s
sections.content: 80 is 'quatre-vingts' (four twenties). Note the 's' at the end of 'vingts'. 81 is 'quatre-vingt-un' (the 's' is dropped when a number follows).

questions.question: How do you say 70 in French?

chapters.title: Numbers 100+
chapters.summary: Scale up to 100, 1000, and millions.

sections.title: 100 and 1000
sections.content: 100: cent. 200: deux cents. (Note the plural 's' if exactly 200/300...). 1000: mille. Mille never takes an 's' even in plural.

chapters.title: Telling Time and Dates
chapters.summary: Tell time, name the days and months, and master date formatting.

sections.title: Telling Time
sections.content: Use 'Il est [hour] heures'. Example: 'Il est trois heures'. Minutes: 'trois heures dix'. Half past: 'trois heures et demie'. Quarter to: 'quatre heures moins le quart'.

sections.title: Days and Months
sections.content: Lundi, Mardi, Mercredi, Jeudi, Vendredi, Samedi, Dimanche. Janvier, Février, Mars, Avril, Mai, Juin, Juillet, Août, Septembre, Octobre, Novembre, Décembre.

examples.english: It is the second of May.

chapters.title: Prepositions of Place
chapters.summary: Describe where things are using precise spatial prepositions.

sections.title: The Big Six
sections.content: Dans (in), Sur (on), Sous (under), Devant (in front of), Derrière (behind), Entre (between).

examples.french: Le chat est sous la table.
examples.english: The cat is under the table.

examples.french: La clé est dans mon sac.
examples.english: The key is in my bag.

questions.question: Which preposition means 'behind'?

chapters.title: Basic Prepositions
chapters.summary: Master the vital connectors: à, de, avec, pour, and sans.

sections.title: A and De
sections.content: À (to/at) and De (of/from) are the most common. Remember they contract with le/les (au, aux, du, des).

sections.title: Avec, Pour, Sans
sections.content: Avec (with), Pour (for), Sans (without).

examples.french: Un café sans sucre, s'il vous plaît.

chapters.title: Ne Dropping in Speech
chapters.summary: Understand the most common shortcut in spoken French: dropping the 'ne'.

sections.title: The disappearing 'Ne'
sections.content: In casual spoken French, the 'ne' part of the 'ne... pas' sandwich is almost always dropped. 'Je ne sais pas' becomes 'Je sais pas'. This is extremely common and standard in informal settings.

examples.english: Don't worry. (Spoken, ne dropped and tu elided)

chapters.title: A1 Consolidation
chapters.summary: Review and unify all core A1 grammar concepts: articles, basic verbs, negation, and numbers.

sections.title: A1 Level Recap
sections.content: Congratulations! You have covered the building blocks of French. To succeed at Level A1, you should be able to: (1) Introduce yourself and others using Être and Avoir. (2) Ask and answer simple questions about numbers, time, and locations. (3) Use definite, indefinite, and partitive articles correctly. (4) Construct simple negative sentences.

examples.french: Bonjour, je suis étudiant et j'ai vingt ans.
examples.english: Hello, I am a student and I am 20 years old.

examples.french: Est-ce que tu as des frères ?

questions.question: Which verb is used for physical feelings like 'hungry' or 'thirsty'?
What is the negative form of 'Ils ont des amis'?

chapters.title: A2 Readiness Checkpoint
chapters.summary: Check your readiness for level A2 with a comprehensive diagnostic review of A1 grammar and communication.

sections.title: Transitioning to A2
sections.content: A2 (Elementary) level moves beyond basic survival French into describing your life, your past, and your opinions. Before starting, confirm you can comfortably use A1 verbs (Être, Avoir, ER/IR/RE), articles, and basic question forms.

questions.question: Which verb is used to say 'I am 20 years old'?

chapters.title: Language Registers Framework
chapters.summary: Understand the three levels of French speech: Familier, Courant, and Soutenu.

sections.title: The Three Registers
sections.content: 1. **Familier**: Spoken with friends/family (uses slang, drops 'ne'). 2. **Courant**: Standard daily French (work, shops, news). 3. **Soutenu**: Formal/Literary (used in speeches and literature).

examples.french: Je ne sais pas (Courant) vs. Je sais pas (Familier)

chapters.title: Tutoiement vs Vouvoiement
chapters.summary: Master the delicate social balance between using 'tu' and 'vous'.

sections.title: When to say 'Tu'
sections.content: Used for children, family, close friends, and fellow students. Switching from 'vous' to 'tu' usually requires a 'social invitation'.

sections.title: When to say 'Vous'
sections.content: Used for anyone you don't know, elders, superiors at work, and plural groups. When in doubt, always start with 'vous'.

examples.french: S'il te plaît (Tu) vs. S'il vous plaît (Vous)

chapters.title: Common Irregular Verbs — Present
chapters.summary: Master the irregular modal verbs: Venir, Vouloir, Pouvoir, and Devoir.

sections.title: Vouloir, Pouvoir, Devoir
sections.content: These 'booster' verbs are followed by an infinitive. **Vouloir** (to want): je veux, tu veux, il veut, nous voulons, vous voulez, ils veulent. **Pouvoir** (to be able to): je peux... **Devoir** (must/have to): je dois...

sections.title: Venir (To Come)
sections.content: Je viens, tu viens, il vient, nous venons, vous venez, ils viennent.

questions.question: Conjugate 'devoir' for 'nous'.

chapters.title: Reflexive Verbs
chapters.summary: Learn how to describe daily routines using reflexive (pronominal) verbs.

sections.title: Reflexive Pronouns
sections.content: Verbs like 'se lever' (to get up) use a reflexive pronoun: je **me**, tu **te**, il/elle **se**, nous **nous**, vous **vous**, ils **se**.

examples.french: Nous nous levons à sept heures.
examples.english: We get up at seven o'clock.

questions.question: Which pronoun is used for 'ils' in reflexive verbs?

chapters.title: Savoir vs Connaître
chapters.summary: Distinguish between 'knowing a fact' and 'being familiar with someone or something'.

sections.title: Savoir (To Know facts/how to)
sections.content: Use 'savoir' for information, facts, or knowing how to do something (followed by a verb). Example: 'Je sais nager.'

sections.title: Connaître (To Know people/places)
sections.content: Use 'connaître' for familiarity with a person, a place, or a thing. It is followed by a noun. Example: 'Je connais Paris.'

examples.english: Do you know where he lives? (Fact)

questions.question: Which verb do you use for 'I know how to drive'?

chapters.title: Partir vs Quitter vs Laisser vs Sortir
chapters.summary: Stop mixing up the four different ways to say 'to leave' in French.

sections.title: Partir vs Quitter
sections.content: **Partir** is intransitive ('I'm leaving'). **Quitter** needs an object ('I'm leaving the house').

sections.title: Sortir vs Laisser
sections.content: **Sortir** means to go out of a room/building. **Laisser** means to leave something behind (e.g., keys).

examples.english: I am going out with my friends.

examples.french: J'ai laissé mon sac à l'école.
examples.english: I left my bag at school.

chapters.title: Passé Composé with Avoir
chapters.summary: Master the primary past tense in French: The Passé Composé with Avoir.

sections.title: The Formula
sections.content: Auxiliary (Avoir) + Past Participle. **-ER** -> -é, **-IR** -> -i, **-RE** -> -u. Example: J'ai mangé, Tu as fini, Il a vendu.

sections.title: Past Participle Agreement with COD
sections.content: When using 'avoir', the past participle normally doesn't agree. However, if a Direct Object (COD) comes BEFORE the verb, the participle MUST agree. 'J'ai mangé la pomme' -> 'La pomme que j'ai mangée'.

sections.title: Negation in Passé Composé
sections.content: Place 'ne' and 'pas' around the auxiliary verb (avoir/être). 'Je n'ai pas mangé'.

questions.question: What is the past participle of 'parler'?
Where does 'pas' go in 'Je n'ai pas mangé'?

chapters.title: Passé Composé with Être
chapters.summary: Learn which verbs must use Être as their auxiliary in the past tense.

sections.title: DR MRS VANDERTRAMP
sections.content: Verbs of motion (Aller, Venir, Entrer, Sortir...) and reflexive verbs use **Être**. Don't forget: the participle must agree with the subject in gender and number! Example: 'Elle est allée'.

questions.question: Which auxiliary does 'Partir' use in the past?

chapters.title: Irregular Past Participles
chapters.summary: A comprehensive guide to the irregular participles you actually need to know.

sections.title: Key Irregulars
sections.content: Faire -> fait, Prendre -> pris, Dire -> dit, Voir -> vu, Vouloir -> voulu, Pouvoir -> pu, Devoir -> dû, Savoir -> su, Boire -> bu, Lire -> lu.

chapters.title: Venir de — Recent Past
chapters.summary: Master the 'Venir de' construction to describe actions that happened just a few moments ago.

sections.title: Formation of the Recent Past
sections.content: The recent past (passé récent) is formed using the present tense of 'venir' + 'de' + an infinitive. It translates to 'to have just done something'. Example: 'Je viens de manger' (I have just eaten).

examples.french: Nous venons de finir nos devoirs.
examples.english: We have just finished our homework.

questions.question: How do you say 'I have just arrived'?

chapters.title: Il y a — Existence and Time
chapters.summary: Learn the double meaning of 'Il y a'—describing existence (there is/are) and time (ago).

sections.title: Existence: There is / There are
sections.content: 'Il y a' is used to show the existence of people or things. It is invariable (does not change for plural). Example: 'Il y a un chat' vs. 'Il y a des chats'.

sections.title: Time: Ago
sections.content: When followed by a time duration, 'il y a' means 'ago'. Example: 'Il y a trois jours' (Three days ago).

examples.french: Il y a une boulangerie ici.
Je l'ai vu il y a deux heures.
examples.english: I saw him two hours ago.

chapters.title: Prepositions with Countries
chapters.summary: Correctly use prepositions (en, au, aux, à) when talking about traveling to countries and cities.

sections.title: Feminine Countries and Continents
sections.content: Use **EN** for all feminine countries (ending in -e, e.g., la France) and all continents. Example: 'En France', 'En Afrique', 'En Europe'.

sections.title: Masculine and Plural Countries
sections.content: Use **AU** for masculine countries (le Canada -> au Canada). Use **AUX** for plural countries (les Etats-Unis -> aux Etats-Unis).

sections.title: Cities
sections.content: Use **À** for almost all cities. Example: 'À Paris', 'À Londres'.

examples.french: Je vais au Japon cet été.
examples.english: I am going to Japan this summer.

questions.question: Which preposition is used for 'United States' (Etats-Unis)?

chapters.title: Basic Conjunctions
chapters.summary: Connect your ideas with basic conjunctions: et, mais, ou, parce que, and donc.

sections.title: Common Conjunctions
sections.content: Et (and), Mais (but), Ou (or), Parce que (because), Donc (so/therefore).

examples.french: Je suis fatigué mais je dois travailler.
examples.english: I am tired but I must work.

examples.french: Il mange parce qu'il a faim.
examples.english: He is eating because he is hungry.

chapters.title: Direct Object Pronouns
chapters.summary: Replace redundant nouns with Direct Object Pronouns (COD) like le, la, and les.

sections.title: What is a COD?
sections.content: A direct object pronoun replaces a noun that answers 'What?' or 'Who?'. Pronouns: **me, te, le, la, nous, vous, les**. Placement: They usually go BEFORE the conjugated verb.

sections.title: COD Placement with Two Verbs
sections.content: When you have two verbs (conjugated + infinitive), the object pronoun usually goes between them. 'Je veux le manger' (I want to eat it).

sections.title: COD in the Imperative
sections.content: In an affirmative command, the pronoun goes AFTER the verb with a hyphen: 'Mange-le !'. In a negative command, it stays before: 'Ne le mange pas !'.

examples.french: Je mange la pomme -> Je la mange.
examples.english: I eat the apple -> I eat it.

questions.question: Where does the pronoun go in 'Je t'aime'?
Where does 'la' go in 'Je veux regarder la télé'?

chapters.title: Indirect Object Pronouns
chapters.summary: Master Indirect Object Pronouns (COI) like lui and leur to replace nouns after 'à'.

sections.title: Lui and Leur
sections.content: COIs replace people after the preposition 'à'. **Lui** (to him/her), **Leur** (to them). Placement: same as COD.

examples.french: Je parle à Marie -> Je lui parle.
examples.english: I speak to Marie -> I speak to her.

examples.french: Je donne les clés aux étudiants -> Je leur donne les clés.
examples.english: I give the keys to the students -> I give them the keys.

questions.question: Which pronoun means 'to them'?

chapters.title: Extended Negation
chapters.summary: Go beyond 'ne... pas' with extended negatives like never, nothing, and no longer.

sections.title: The Many 'Pas' Replacements
sections.content: Replace 'pas' with: **plus** (no longer), **jamais** (never), **rien** (nothing), **personne** (no one). Example: 'Je ne bois plus' (I no longer drink).

questions.question: Which negative means 'never'?

chapters.title: Vowel Dropping
chapters.summary: Learn the elision rules for 'ce', 'que', and 'si' to sound like a native.

sections.title: Dropping Vowels
sections.content: Ce becomes c' before a vowel (c'est). Que becomes qu' (qu'il). **Si** only elides before **il/ils** (s'il, s'ils), never before elle.

chapters.title: Stressed Pronouns
chapters.summary: Use stressed pronouns (moi, toi, lui...) after prepositions and for emphasis.

sections.title: The Stressed Forms
sections.content: Moi, toi, lui, elle, nous, vous, eux, elles. Use after prepositions (avec moi) or for emphasis (Moi, je préfère le thé).

chapters.title: Imperatives
chapters.summary: Learn how to give commands, advice, and suggestions using the Imperative mood.

sections.title: Forming the Imperial
sections.content: Use the Tu, Nous, or Vous form but DROP the subject pronoun. Note: For -ER verbs, drop the 's' for the Tu form. Example: 'Parle!' (Speak!).

questions.question: Which imperative form means 'Let's eat'?

chapters.title: Ce vs Ça vs Cela
chapters.summary: Master the demonstrative pronouns Ce, Ça, and Cela for referring to ideas and things.

sections.title: Ce vs Ça
sections.content: 'Ce' is used with the verb être (c'est). 'Ça' is the contraction of 'cela' used in spoken French as a general object pronoun. Example: 'C'est beau' vs 'J'aime ça'.

chapters.title: Possessive Adjectives
chapters.summary: Express ownership using possessive adjectives (mon, ton, son...).

sections.title: Agreement
sections.content: Possessive adjectives agree with the thing possessed, not the owner. Mon/ma/mes, ton/ta/tes, son/sa/ses. Note: use 'mon' before feminine words starting with a vowel (mon amie).

chapters.title: Demonstrative Adjectives
chapters.summary: Point things out with demonstrative adjectives: ce, cette, and ces.

sections.title: Forms
sections.content: Ce (m.), Cette (f.), Ces (pl.). Use 'Cet' for masculine singular before a vowel (cet homme).

chapters.title: C'est vs Il Est (Advanced)
chapters.summary: Master the advanced nuances between 'C'est' and 'Il/Elle est' for complex identification and descriptions.

sections.title: C'est with Modified Nouns
sections.content: Even for professions, if you add an adjective to the noun, you must switch back to 'c'est'. For example, while you say 'Il est médecin' (He is a doctor), you must say 'C'est un excellent médecin' (He is an excellent doctor).

sections.title: General vs Specific Statements
sections.content: Use 'C'est' + masculine singular adjective for general statements: 'La vie en France, c'est fantastique !' Use 'Il/Elle est' for specific things: 'La tour Eiffel ? Elle est magnifique !'

examples.french: Le français, c'est difficile mais intéressant.

questions.question: Choose the correct starting phrase: '___ une actrice célèbre.'
questions.explanation: Because 'actrice' is modified by the adjective 'célèbre' and has an article 'une', you must use 'c'est'.

chapters.title: Interrogative Adjective Quel
chapters.summary: Use the interrogative adjective 'quel' to ask 'which' or 'what' with precise gender and number agreement.

sections.title: Forms of Quel
sections.content: Quel must agree with the noun it modifies. Masculine singular: quel. Feminine singular: quelle. Masculine plural: quels. Feminine plural: quelles. They all sound exactly the same!

sections.title: Usage with Être
sections.content: Quel can be separated from its noun by the verb être. Example: 'Quelles sont tes idées ?' (What are your ideas?)

examples.french: Le matin, tu bois quel café ?
examples.english: In the morning, which coffee do you drink?

chapters.title: Comparative and Superlative
chapters.summary: Compare items and express the 'best' or 'worst' using comparatives and superlatives.

sections.title: Comparative
sections.content: Plus... que (more), Moins... que (less), Aussi... que (as). Superlative: Le plus... (the most), Le moins... (the least).

chapters.title: Adverbs
chapters.summary: Transform adjectives into adverbs by adding the suffix -ment, and master the exceptions.

sections.title: The General Rule
sections.content: To form an adverb, take the feminine singular form of an adjective and add '-ment'. Example: lent -> lente -> lentement (slowly). Heureux -> heureuse -> heureusement (fortunately).

sections.title: Vowel Endings
sections.content: If the masculine adjective already ends in a vowel, just add '-ment' to the masculine form. Example: Vrai -> Vraiment (truly). Absolu -> Absolument.

sections.title: The -ant / -ent irregulars
sections.content: Adjectives ending in -ant or -ent drop the 'nt' and add '-mment'. The 'e' in -emment is pronounced like an 'a'. Example: évident -> évidemment, constant -> constamment.

chapters.title: Discourse Markers
chapters.summary: Placeholder summary for Discourse Markers.

sections.title: Understanding Discourse Markers
sections.content: The topic of Discourse Markers is a key component of the A2 syllabus. It involves understanding the structural rules and stylistic applications that allow for more precise and natural communication in French.

chapters.title: On vs Nous
chapters.summary: Understand the conversational shift in French where 'on' replaces 'nous' in 90% of daily speech.

sections.title: On vs Nous in Speech
sections.content: In standard written French, 'nous' means 'we'. In conversational French, 'on' is almost exclusively used to mean 'we'. 'On y va' is much more common than 'Nous y allons'.

sections.title: Conjugation Mismatch
sections.content: Even though 'on' translates to 'we' in meaning, it always takes the 3rd person singular verb conjugation (like il/elle). However, adjectives associated with 'on' will be plural! Example: 'On est fatigués' (We are tired).

examples.english: We are ready to leave. (Note the plural 's' on prêts)

chapters.title: Pronunciation Refinement
chapters.summary: Placeholder summary for Pronunciation Refinement.

sections.title: Understanding Pronunciation Refinement
sections.content: The topic of Pronunciation Refinement is a key component of the A2 syllabus. It involves understanding the structural rules and stylistic applications that allow for more precise and natural communication in French.

chapters.title: Imparfait Intro
chapters.summary: Discover the past tense of description and habit: the Imparfait.

sections.title: Formation
sections.content: Take the 'nous' form of the present, drop -ons, and add: -ais, -ais, -ait, -ions, -iez, -aient. Exception: être (ét-).

chapters.title: Futur Proche
chapters.summary: Talk about the immediate future using Aller + Infinitive.

sections.title: Futur Proche
sections.content: Conjugate 'aller' in the present + the infinitive of your action. Example: 'Je vais manger' (I am going to eat).

chapters.title: A2 Consolidation
chapters.summary: Placeholder summary for A2 Consolidation.

sections.title: Understanding A2 Consolidation
sections.content: The topic of A2 Consolidation is a key component of the A2 syllabus. It involves understanding the structural rules and stylistic applications that allow for more precise and natural communication in French.

chapters.title: B1 Readiness Checkpoint
chapters.summary: Placeholder summary for B1 Readiness Checkpoint.

sections.title: Understanding B1 Readiness Checkpoint
sections.content: The topic of B1 Readiness Checkpoint is a key component of the B1 syllabus. It involves understanding the structural rules and stylistic applications that allow for more precise and natural communication in French.

examples.french: (Example of B1 Readiness Checkpoint in context)

chapters.title: Imparfait — Full Usage
chapters.summary: Use the Imparfait to describe background settings, habits, and ongoing past actions.

sections.title: Usage
sections.content: Use for: 1. Habitual actions (I used to go). 2. Descriptions (The sun was shining). 3. Ongoing states (I was tired).

chapters.title: Imparfait vs Passé Composé
chapters.summary: Solve the biggest challenge in French: choosing between Imparfait and Passé Composé.

sections.title: The Rule of Thumb
sections.content: Passé Composé is for specific, finished actions (The interruption). Imparfait is for the background or habit (The stage). Example: 'Je regardais la télé (imp.) quand le téléphone a sonné (pc).'

chapters.title: Plus-que-parfait
chapters.summary: Learn to express 'the past in the past' using the Plus-que-parfait (Pluperfect) tense.

sections.title: Formation
sections.content: The plus-que-parfait translates to 'had done'. Form it using the Imparfait of the auxiliary (avoir/être) + the Past Participle. Example: 'J'avais mangé' (I had eaten), 'Il était parti' (He had left).

sections.title: Usage context
sections.content: Use it alongside the Passé Composé when recounting stories to clarify the sequence of events. Example: 'Quand je suis arrivé, le train était déjà parti' (When I arrived, the train had already left).

examples.french: Il m'a dit qu'il avait vu ce film.
examples.english: He told me that he had seen this movie.

chapters.title: Future Simple
chapters.summary: Talk about intentions and long-term plans using the Future Simple.

sections.title: Future Simple
sections.content: Form: Infinitive + -ai, -as, -a, -ons, -ez, -ont. IR/ER keep the final 'r'. RE drop the final 'e' before adding endings.

chapters.title: Conditional Present
chapters.summary: Express desires, polite requests, and hypothetical scenarios with the Conditional.

sections.title: Formation
sections.content: Take the Future stem + Imparfait endings. Example: 'Je voudrais' (I would like).

chapters.title: Relative Pronouns
chapters.summary: Create complex sentences using relative pronouns Qui, Que, Dont, and Où.

sections.title: Qui, Que, Dont, Où
sections.content: Qui (subject), Que (direct object), Dont (replaces 'de'), Où (place/time).

chapters.title: Si Clauses
chapters.summary: Express conditions and hypotheses using 'Si' clauses.

sections.title: Tense Sequences
sections.content: 1. Si + Present -> Future. 2. Si + Imparfait -> Conditional.

chapters.title: Y and En
chapters.summary: Master the versatile pronouns Y (there) and En (some/of it).

sections.title: Y and En
sections.content: Y replaces 'à + place/thing'. En replaces 'de + thing/quantity'. Example: 'J'en veux' (I want some). 'J'y vais' (I'm going there).

chapters.title: Double Object Pronouns
chapters.summary: Learn the strict order of multiple pronouns in a single sentence.

sections.title: Pronoun Order
sections.content: Order: Me/Te/Se -> Le/La/Les -> Lui/Leur -> Y -> En. Example: 'Je le lui donne' (I give it to him).

chapters.title: Subjunctive Intro
chapters.summary: Introduction to the mood of doubt, desire, and emotion: the Subjunctive.

sections.title: When to use Subjunctive
sections.content: Used after 'que' when expressing: 1. Will/Desire (Je veux que...). 2. Emotion (Je suis content que...). 3. Doubt (Je doute que...).

chapters.title: Passive Voice
chapters.summary: Construct sentences in the passive voice to shift focus from the doer to the receiver of the action.

sections.title: Formation of the Passive
sections.content: The passive voice uses the verb 'être' (conjugated in the desired tense) + the Past Participle. The participle always agrees with the subject. Example: 'La souris est mangée par le chat' (The mouse is eaten by the cat).

sections.title: Par vs De
sections.content: The agent is usually introduced by 'par' (by). However, verbs of emotion or state (aimer, respecter, couvrir) use 'de'. 'Elle est aimée de tous' (She is loved by everyone).

examples.french: La loi a été votée par le parlement.
examples.english: The law was passed by the parliament.

chapters.title: Reported Speech
chapters.summary: Master the complex tense shifts required when reporting someone else's speech.

sections.title: Present Intro Verbs vs Past Intro Verbs
sections.content: If the introductory verb (He says...) is in the present, no tenses change: Il dit, 'Je suis malade' -> Il dit qu'il est malade. If intro verb is in the past, a shift is triggered!

sections.title: The Sequence of Tenses
sections.content: When intro verb is past (Il a dit...): Present -> Imparfait. Passé Composé -> Plus-que-parfait. Futur -> Conditionnel. Example: 'Je viendrai' -> Il a dit qu'il viendrait.

examples.french: Elle a dit: 'J'ai fini mon livre.'
Elle a dit qu'elle avait fini son livre.
examples.english: She said that she had finished her book.

chapters.title: Impersonal Expressions
chapters.summary: Placeholder summary for Impersonal Expressions.

sections.title: Understanding Impersonal Expressions
sections.content: The topic of Impersonal Expressions is a key component of the B1 syllabus. It involves understanding the structural rules and stylistic applications that allow for more precise and natural communication in French.

chapters.title: B1 Consolidation
chapters.summary: Review your B1 Intermediate mastery and prepare for the B2 Upper Intermediate level.

sections.title: B1 Level Conclusion
sections.content: You are now an independent user. You can handle most travel situations, describe events in various tenses, and express opinions with supporting reasons.

chapters.title: B2 Readiness Checkpoint
chapters.summary: Review and assess your progress in the B2 level syllabus.

sections.title: Level Review
sections.content: A diagnostic summary of your current progress in B2 French grammar.

chapters.title: Subjunctive Deep Dive
chapters.summary: Master the complex nuances of the Subjunctive mood, specifically for necessity, doubt, and emotion in advanced contexts.

sections.title: Subjunctive of Doubt and Negation
sections.content: In B2 French, the subjunctive is often triggered by negation or questioning. While 'Je pense qu'il est là' (Indicative) shows certainty, 'Je ne pense pas qu'il **soit** là' (Subjunctive) introduces doubt. Similarly, 'Crois-tu qu'il **vienne** ?' requires the subjunctive for uncertainty.

sections.title: Conjunction Triggers
sections.content: Master these essential B2 conjunctions that always take the subjunctive: **bien que** (although), **quoique** (even though), **pour que / afin que** (so that), **avant que** (before), and **à moins que** (unless — often used with the 'ne explétif').

examples.french: Bien qu'il fasse froid, nous sortons.
examples.english: Even though it's cold, we are going out.

examples.french: Je partirai à moins qu'il ne pleuve.
examples.english: I will leave unless it rains.

questions.question: Which conjunction does NOT take the subjunctive?
questions.explanation: Technically, 'après que' describes an action that has already happened (fact), so it takes the indicative. However, many natives use the subjunctive in speech.

chapters.title: Complex Relative Clauses
chapters.summary: Complex relative pronouns (lequel, auquel, duquel) allow for precise descriptions after prepositions.

sections.title: Forms and Agreement
sections.content: Unlike qui/que, complex relative pronouns agree with the gender and number of the antecedent: lequel (m.s.), laquelle (f.s.), lesquels (m.p.), lesquelles (f.p.).

sections.title: Contractions with À and De
sections.content: When preceded by 'à' or 'de', they contract: à + lequel = **auquel**, de + lequel = **duquel**. Note that 'dont' is often preferred for simple 'de' relations, but 'duquel' is used after compound prepositions like 'à côté de'.

examples.french: C'est l'entreprise pour laquelle je travaille.
examples.english: It is the company for which I work.

examples.french: L'immeuble à côté duquel j'habite est très vieux.
examples.english: The building next to which I live is very old.

chapters.title: Tout Usage
chapters.summary: Master the complex agreements of 'tout' as a pronoun, adjective, and adverb.

sections.title: Tout as an Adjective
sections.content: When used as an adjective modifying a noun, it must agree in gender and number: tout, toute, tous, toutes. Example: 'Tous les jours' (Every day).

sections.title: Tout as a Pronoun
sections.content: Replacing a noun: 'Tous sont venus' (Everyone came). Note: When 'tous' is a pronoun, the final 's' is pronounced! ([tus]).

sections.title: Tout as an Adverb
sections.content: Meaning 'entirely' or 'very', it is usually invariable: 'Elles sont tout étonnées.' Exception: It agrees before a feminine adjective starting with a consonant or aspirated h: 'Elle est toute petite.'

examples.english: She was completely ashamed. (Adverb agreeing with feminine aspirated h)

questions.question: Is 'tout' variable or invariable in 'Elle est tout émue'?
questions.explanation: 'Emue' starts with a vowel, so the adverb 'tout' is invariable.

chapters.title: Même Usage
chapters.summary: Distinguish between 'même' as an adjective (the same) and 'même' as an adverb (even).

sections.title: Même as an Adjective
sections.content: When placed before a noun, it means 'the same' and agrees in number. Example: 'Nous avons les mêmes problèmes' (We have the same problems).

sections.title: Même as an Adverb
sections.content: When modifying a verb or an adjective, it means 'even' and is invariable. Example: 'Même les enfants le savent' (Even the children know it).

examples.french: Il a refusé, et même il s'est fâché.
examples.english: He refused, and he even got angry.

chapters.title: Advanced Passive Voice
chapters.summary: Understand advanced passive structures, including 'se faire' and 'se laisser'.

sections.title: The Pronominal Passive
sections.content: To avoid the heavy structure of 'être + past participle', French often uses reflexive verbs for passive meanings. Example: 'Ça se dit beaucoup ici' (That is said a lot here).

sections.title: Se faire and Se laisser
sections.content: Used when the subject is the victim or allows an action to happen to them. Example: 'Il s'est fait voler sa voiture' (He had his car stolen). 'Elle s'est laissé convaincre' (She let herself be convinced).

chapters.title: Faire + Infinitive
chapters.summary: Use the causative 'faire + infinitif' to express actions done by someone else at your request.

sections.title: The Structure
sections.content: Form: Subject + Faire (conjugated) + Infinitive + [Object]. The subject does not perform the action but causes it. Example: 'Le professeur fait lire les élèves' (The teacher makes the students read).

examples.french: Je me fais couper les cheveux.
Il a fait construire une maison.

chapters.title: Indirect Speech Advanced
chapters.summary: Master the complex chronological shifts required when reporting speech in the past sequence.

sections.title: Rigid Tense Shifts
sections.content: When the reporting verb is past (Il a dit que...), the original statement shifts back in time. Present -> Imparfait. Passé Composé -> Plus-que-parfait. Futur -> Conditionnel. Futur Antérieur -> Conditionnel Passé.

sections.title: Time Markers
sections.content: Time expressions must also shift! Aujourd'hui -> Ce jour-là. Hier -> La veille. Demain -> Le lendemain. Example: 'Je viendrai demain' -> Il a dit qu'il viendrait le lendemain.

examples.french: Il m'a affirmé qu'il l'avait vue la veille.
examples.english: He assured me that he had seen her the day before.

chapters.title: Nominalisation
chapters.summary: Nominalisation is the art of turning verbal phrases into nouns to sound more professional and concise.

sections.title: Why Nominalize?
sections.content: In administrative and academic French, nouns are preferred over verbs. It makes sentences more objective and dense. Example: 'Il a démissionné' (He resigned) becomes 'Sa démission' (His resignation).

examples.french: La fermeture de l'usine a surpris tout le monde.
examples.english: The closing of the factory surprised everyone.

chapters.title: Nuanced Article Usage
chapters.summary: Recognize when to omit articles entirely for literary effect or fixed professional phrases.

sections.title: Absence of Article
sections.content: Articles are omitted in proverbs (Pierre qui roule n'amasse pas mousse), enumerations in literature, and after certain prepositions like 'sans' or 'avec' indicating an abstract manner: 'Il parle avec passion'.

chapters.title: Sequence of Tenses
chapters.summary: Understand the strict rules of 'Concordance des temps' for complex logical sentences.

sections.title: Sequence of Tenses
sections.content: If the main verb is in the past, subordinate verbs often shift (e.g., to Plus-que-parfait or Conditionnel Passé).

chapters.title: Argumentation Markers
chapters.summary: Argumentation markers are the glue of B2/C1 discourse, allowing for structured debate and analysis.

sections.title: Logical Progressors
sections.content: Use sophisticated markers to structure your points: **Certes** (granted), **Toutefois** (however), **Par ailleurs** (furthermore), **En revanche** (on the other hand), **En somme** (in short).
