chapters.summary: Review and assess your progress in the B2 level syllabus.

sections.title: Level Review
sections.content: A diagnostic summary of your current progress in B2 French grammar.

chapters.title: C1 Readiness Checkpoint
chapters.summary: Review and assess your progress in the C1 level syllabus.

sections.title: Level Review
sections.content: A diagnostic summary of your current progress in C1 French grammar.

chapters.title: Passé Simple
chapters.summary: The Passé Simple is the essential written past tense for literature, history, and formal storytelling.

sections.title: Identification and Usage
sections.content: While rarely spoken, the Passé Simple is ubiquitous in French literature (from Balzac to Modiano). It functions like the Passé Composé but indicates a high/literary register.

sections.title: Common Forms
sections.content: ER verbs: -ai, -as, -a, -âmes, -âtes, -èrent. IR/RE verbs: -is, -is, -it, -îmes, -îtes, -irent. Irregular: fut (être), eut (avoir), vit (voir).

examples.french: Elle fut surprise par la nouvelle.
examples.english: She was surprised by the news. (Formal/Written)

chapters.title: Imparfait du Subjonctif
chapters.summary: The rarest mood: Master the Imparfait du Subjonctif in formal written contexts.

sections.title: Formal Subjunctive
sections.content: Used in high literature after a main verb in the past. Example: 'Il fallut qu'il partît' (It was necessary that he leave).

chapters.title: Oral vs Written Grammar
chapters.summary: Navigate the profound structural differences between spoken colloquialisms and formal written French.

sections.title: Structural Simplification
sections.content: In oral French, 'nous' becomes 'on', 'ne' is dropped, and questions are formed purely by intonation. Written French maintains strict inversion, full negations, and complex relative clauses.

sections.title: Lexical Elevation
sections.content: C1 mastery requires knowing the formal equivalent of everyday words. 'Faire' becomes 'effectuer' or 'réaliser'. 'Mettre' becomes 'placer' or 'insérer'.

chapters.title: Formal Written French
chapters.summary: Master the protocols and linguistic precision of formal written French.

sections.title: Precision and Register
sections.content: Formal French avoids 'on' in favor of 'nous' or the passive voice. It utilizes complex logical connectors and precise vocabulary (e.g., 'subséquemment' instead of 'après').

chapters.title: Advanced Hypothesis Structures
chapters.summary: Formulate highly complex hypothetical scenarios using inverted 'Si' clauses and the second conditional past.

sections.title: Le Conditionnel Passé - 2ème Forme
sections.content: In lofty literature, the conditionnel passé is replaced by the plus-que-parfait du subjonctif. Example: 'J'eusse aimé' instead of 'J'aurais aimé' (I would have liked).

sections.title: Inverting 'Si'
sections.content: In very formal writing, you can drop 'Si' entirely by inverting the subject and verb in the pluperfect. 'Si j'avais su...' becomes 'Eussé-je su...'

examples.french: Fût-il arrivé plus tôt, la tragédie eût été évitée.
examples.english: Had he arrived earlier, the tragedy would have been avoided. (Extremely formal)

chapters.title: Grammar of Persuasion
chapters.summary: Examine the grammar of persuasion: using the 'ne' littéraire, passive structures, and fronting for emphasis.

sections.title: Le Ne Littéraire
sections.content: In high-register French, some verbs (cessez, oser, pouvoir, savoir) can be made negative using ONLY 'ne', omitting 'pas'. Example: 'Je ne saurais vous dire' (I couldn't tell you).

examples.english: He doesn't dare speak of it. (Formal rhetorical negative)

chapters.title: Subtle Tense Distinctions
chapters.summary: Understand the profound stylistic differences between the Imparfait, Passé Simple, and Passé Antérieur.

sections.title: The Temporal Hierarchy
sections.content: In a literary text, the Passé Simple drives the plot forward (punctual events), the Imparfait paints the background (states/durations), and the Passé Antérieur establishes events completed immediately before a Passé Simple action.

chapters.summary: Review and assess your progress in the C1 level syllabus.

sections.title: Level Review
sections.content: A diagnostic summary of your current progress in C1 French grammar.

chapters.title: C2 Readiness Checkpoint
chapters.summary: Review and assess your progress in the C2 level syllabus.

sections.title: Level Review
sections.content: A diagnostic summary of your current progress in C2 French grammar.

chapters.title: Complete Tense Mastery
chapters.summary: Attain absolute mastery over French tenses, recognizing subtle aspectual differences native speakers use naturally.

sections.title: Anterior Tenses Overview
sections.content: Advanced French uses anterior tenses to show relationships in time explicitly: Passé antérieur (Il eut fini), Futur antérieur (J'aurai terminé), and Conditionnel passé (J'aurais voulu). Understanding when to deploy these elevates writing to native logic.

sections.title: Aspect vs. Tense
sections.content: At C2 level, you must master 'aspect'—the duration or completion of an action. Distinguishing between 'Il peignait la porte' (He was painting the door - process) and 'Il a peint la porte' (He painted the door - completion).

examples.french: Dès qu'il eut achevé son repas, il se leva.
examples.english: As soon as he had finished his meal, he stood up. (Passé antérieur - Literary)

chapters.title: Register Switching
chapters.summary: Achieve native-level social flexibility by seamlessly switching between street slang (argot) and highly formal registers.

sections.title: L'Argot and Verlan
sections.content: To sound like a local, C2 speakers understand 'verlan' (inverting syllables: femme -> meuf, fou -> ouf). They drop the 'il' in spoken expressions ('Faut qu'on y aille' instead of 'Il faut que nous y allions').

sections.title: Code-Switching Dynamics
sections.content: True mastery is knowing exactly when to drop verlan at a job interview and switch instantly to 'soutenu'. This requires a deep cultural radar, recognizing the implicit social hierarchy in any setting.

chapters.title: Stylistic Grammar Mastery
chapters.summary: Elevate your French to an artistic level by deploying figures of speech, inversions, and rhetorical devices.

sections.title: Litotes and Euphemisms
sections.content: French culture values indirectness. A litote asserts something by denying its opposite: 'Ce n'est pas mal' (It's not bad = It's great). This understatement is a hallmark of native speech.

sections.title: Inversion for Style
sections.content: In literature or formal speeches, subject-verb inversion occurs outside of questions. After 'Peut-être' or 'Aussi', inversion is required: 'Aussi avons-nous décidé...' (Thus we have decided...).

chapters.title: Grammar in Literature
chapters.summary: Analyze how celebrated French authors bend grammatical rules for poetic and artistic impact.

sections.title: La Liberté Littéraire
sections.content: Authors often employ chiasmus, zeugma, or deliberate anacoluthon (syntactical dissonance) to jar the reader or create profound rhythm.

sections.title: Historical Syntax
sections.content: Understanding archaic sentence structures, such as the pre-positioning of adjectives or the historical use of 'ne' without 'pas', is essential for accessing classic works like Racine or Hugo.

examples.french: Vêtu de probité candide et de lin blanc. (Victor Hugo)
examples.english: Clothed in candid probity and white linen. (Zeugma: mixing abstract and concrete)

chapters.title: Legal French
chapters.summary: Decipher and produce the specialized structures of French legal, administrative, and diplomatic documents.

sections.title: Le Langage Administratif
sections.content: Administrative French (Adminstrativeese) heavily relies on nominalization, the passive voice, and complex bureaucratic vocabulary ('Le formulaire dûment rempli', 'Dans l'attente de votre réponse').

sections.title: Legal Prepositions and Clauses
sections.content: Legal French uses specific prepositions and conditional phrasing to convey absolute objectivity. Example: 'Sous réserve de...' (Subject to...) or 'Conformément à...' (In accordance with...).

examples.french: Je vous prie de bien vouloir agréer, Madame, l'expression de mes salutations distinguées.
examples.english: Please accept, Madam, the expression of my distinguished greetings.

chapters.title: Regional Variation
chapters.summary: Expand your linguistic perspective by understanding the variations of French spoken across the global Francophonie.

sections.title: Le Français Québécois
sections.content: Quebec French retains older vocabulary and pronunciation patterns. Features include: 'tu' added for emphasis in questions ('C'est-tu prêt?'), unique vocabulary ('un char' = car, 'courriel' = email), and distinct vowel qualities.

sections.title: Swiss, Belgian, and African Nuances
sections.content: In Switzerland and Belgium, seventy and ninety simplify to 'septante' and 'nonante' (and 'huitante' in some Swiss cantons). African French variations often innovate rapidly with colorful expressions and localized slang.

chapters.title: Final Mastery Review
chapters.summary: The Final Review: Confirm your mastery of all 151 topics in the French Grammar database.

sections.title: Full Mastery Review
sections.content: You have reached the peak of French grammar. From basic phonetics to complex legal stylistics, you now possess a native-level understanding of the language system.
